english: ṅaṅ-rgyúd (ngang rgyud) id., ṅaṅ-rgyúd bzaṅ-po (ngang rgyud bzang po) Wdṅ., dgé-ba (dge ba) Glr., a naturally good, virtuous character; very frq.: ṅaṅ-rgyúd riṅ-ba (ngang rgyud ring ba) forbearing, longsuffering, not easily put into a passion Glr.; not easily excited to action, phlegmatic, cool, also *ṅaṅ riṅ-wa* (ngang ring wa) C.; even ṅaṅ (ngang) alone may have this meaning: ṅaṅ ma tuṅ (ngang ma thung) don't lose your patience Mil. nt.; ṅaṅ-gis (ngang gis) adv. not only signifies spontaneously, of one's own accord, but also slowly, gradually, gently Mil. (so already Schr.) — 2. dominion, sphere, province, parallel to kloṅ (klong) and dbyiṅs (dbyings) Mil.; *ṅa ma-ši-kai ṅaṅ-la dug* (nga ma shi ka'i ngang la dug) I belong to the kingdom of Christ, said one of our Christians, in order to show the meaning of *ṅaṅ* (ngang). Hence it might be used for expressing the ἐν of the N. T. (I John 5, 6 and many other passages) denoting a pertaining to, belonging to, being connected with. ṅaṅ-la jóg-pa (bźág-pa) (ngang la jog pa (bzhag pa)) Mil. and C. is an expression not explained as yet.

1. goose, more accurately ṅaṅ-pa (ngang pa) a gander, ṅaṅ-ma (ngang ma) a goose Cs. The domestic goose and the breeding of it is not yet known in Tibet, at least not in W. — 2. a light-bay horse, an isabel-coloured horse Ld.-Glr.

1. cog. to ṅá-ra (nga ra), air, *ṅád-la skám-ċe* (ngad la skam ce) W. to dry in the air; in a general sense the air in its chemical qualities, in its influence on the senses: scent, fragrance, spós-kyi ṅad ldáṅ-ba (spos kyi ngad ldang ba) the rising of an aromatic breeze; ṅad yal (ngad yal) the fragrancy, the aroma evaporates; vapour, ká-ṅad (kha ngad), čú-ṅad (chu ngad) snowy vapour, aqueous vapour; aromati csubstance, sṅó-ṅad (sngo ngad) aromatic vegetables, such as onions Med.; cold air, the cold, coldness, v. ṅad-ċan (ngad can). — 2. W. (cf. ṅár-ba (ngar ba), ṅár-ma (ngar ma)) severity, roughness, *ṅe ṅad j́igs dug* (nge ngad 'jigs dug) he fears I might address him harshly; ṅád-ċan (ngad can) 1. fragrant, fresh, cool, W. cold. 2. W. rough, impetuous.

1. evil, mischief, misfortune, ṅan ċén-po byas (ngan chen po byas) it has done great mischief Glr.; esp. harm done by sorcery and witchcraft Mil.; ṅan-dgú (ngan dgu) every possible evil Lt. — 2. curse, imprecation, ṅan ˳debs-pa (ngan 'debs pa), W. *táb - ċe* (thab ce), to curse, to execrate; mtu ṅan ˳débs-pa (mthu ngan 'debs pa) to curse by means of witchcraft. Cf. mṅan (mngan).

col. also *ṅán-po* (ngan po), bad, of food etc.; mean, miserable Dzl.; poor, humble, low, (prop. rigs-ṅán (rigs ngan)), ṅan-lóṅ (ngan long) poor and blind (people) Glr.; lo ṅán-pa (lo ngan pa) a year yielding no crops, an unfruitful or bad year; of men, actions etc.: wicked, ṅán-pa kyoḍ γnyis (ngan pa khyod gnyis) ye two villains! Glr.; noisome, pernicious, γsol-ṅán (gsol ngan) pernicious food, i.e. poison, resp., Glr.; ... la ṅán-du rjód-pa (la ngan du rjod pa) to revile, blaspheme; mí-la mig ṅán ltá-ba (mi la mig ngan lta ba) to look with an evil or envious eye upon a person Glr.; raṅ-rnams spyod ṅan byás-nas dus ṅan zer (rang rnams spyod ngan byas nas dus ngan zer) acting badly themselves they speak of bad times Ma. — ṅan-gro (ngan 'gro), ṅan-soṅ (ngan song) v. gro-ba ('gro ba) I. extr. — ṅan-ṅón (ngan ngon) Cs. mean, pitiful, very bad. — ṅan-ṅón tsám-gyis ćog śés-pa (ngan ngon tsam gyis chog shes pa) prob.: to be satisfied with any thing, and be it ever so poor. — ṅan-ne-ba (ngan ne ba) bad. — *ṅan-p'e* (ngan phe) W. meal of parched barley, roasted meal. — ṅán-so (ngan so) 'bad place', hell; cf. ṅán-gro (ngan 'gro) under gró-ba ('gro ba) I. extr.
C., we, eleg., = bdag (bdag), when speaking humbly of one's self.

Cs. 'torrent', Sch. 'ditch filled with water, water-ditch; the bank of a river grown high and steep by having been gradually washed out by the current'; (only this latter sense of the word was authenticated to me). In Glr. Tibet is poetically called 'ṅam-grog-ċé' (ngam grog che), which is a very appropriate name when rendered: having large and deep erosions.
given to gluttony and drinking Stg.

1. fore- or front-side, forepart, ṅar-vdóṅ (ngar gdong) id.; esp. of the leg, the shin-bone, also knuckle ni f.; lag-ṅár (lag ngar), rkaṅ-ṅár (rkang ngar) forearm, lower part of the leg; rj́e-ṅar (rje ngar) seems to be an appellation for both, (in W. *nyar* (nyar) instead of it). — 2 v. ṅár-ba (ngar ba) 1. —
